seed_instruction: Write a semester-long plan for conducting a study on the impact of social media on mental health among teenagers.
org_constraint: Adopt a third-person perspective throughout the plan.
new_constraint: Adopt a first-person perspective throughout the plan.
instruction: Write a semester-long plan for conducting a study on the impact of social media on mental health among teenagers. Adopt a third-person perspective throughout the plan. Adopt a first-person perspective throughout the plan.
response:
Here are two versions of a semester-long research plan. The first is written in the **third-person perspective** (objective/academic), and the second is written in the **first-person perspective** (personal/reflective).

---

### Option 1: Third-Person Perspective
**Project Title:** *Digital Echoes: A Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis of Social Media Usage and Adolescent Psychological Well-being.*

**Phase I: Foundation and Literature Review (Weeks 1–4)**
The researcher will begin the semester by conducting an extensive review of existing literature regarding social media algorithms, dopamine loops, and adolescent brain development. During this period, the researcher will identify key variables, such as "screen time," "social comparison," and "cyberbullying." By the end of Week 4, a formal research question and a theoretical framework will be established.

**Phase II: Methodology and Ethics (Weeks 5–8)**
The researcher will design the data collection instruments, including a standardized survey and a set of semi-structured interview questions. During this phase, the researcher will submit the proposal to the Institutional Review Board (IRB) to ensure ethical compliance regarding minor participants. The researcher will also select a target demographic of teenagers aged 13–18.

**Phase III: Data Collection (Weeks 9–13)**
The researcher will distribute the surveys via school partnerships and social media platforms. Simultaneously, the researcher will conduct one-on-one interviews with a selected subset of participants. Data will be logged systematically, and the researcher will monitor for any signs of distress in participants, ensuring a safe environment for disclosure.

**Phase IV: Analysis and Synthesis (Weeks 14–15)**
The researcher will utilize statistical software to analyze quantitative survey data and thematic coding to interpret qualitative interview transcripts. The final weeks will be dedicated to synthesizing these findings into a comprehensive research paper, culminating in a final presentation of the correlation between specific social media behaviors and mental health outcomes.

---

### Option 2: First-Person Perspective
**Project Title:** *My Study on the Impact of Social Media on Teen Mental Health.*

**Phase I: Foundation and Literature Review (Weeks 1–4)**
I will start my semester by diving into the current research on how social media affects the teenage brain. I plan to read as many peer-reviewed journals as possible to understand the link between "likes" and self-esteem. By the end of the first month, I will have my research question finalized and a clear roadmap for how I want to approach my study.

**Phase II: Methodology and Ethics (Weeks 5–8)**
Next, I will focus on building my tools. I need to create a survey that is easy for my peers to understand but detailed enough to give me good data. I will also spend time drafting my ethics application; since I am studying minors, I have to be extremely careful about privacy and consent. I want to make sure my participants feel safe sharing their honest experiences with me.

**Phase III: Data Collection (Weeks 9–13)**
This is the most active part of my semester. I will be reaching out to local schools and using my own networks to get the word out about my survey. I also plan to sit down with a few students for one-on-one interviews. I want to hear their personal stories—not just numbers on a page. I will keep a close eye on the data as it comes in to ensure I’m capturing the nuances of their daily lives.

**Phase IV: Analysis and Synthesis (Weeks 14–15)**
In the final stretch, I will sit down with all the data I’ve gathered. I’ll use software to crunch the numbers from the surveys and look for recurring themes in my interviews. My goal is to connect the dots between what the data says and what the teenagers told me. I will spend the last two weeks polishing my final paper and preparing to present my findings to the class.<turn|>

---

### Option 1: Third-Person Perspective
**Project Title:** *Digital Echoes: A Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis of Social Media Usage and Adolescent Psychological Well-being.*

**Phase I: Foundation and Literature Review (Weeks 1–4)**

During this period, the researcher will identify key variables, such as "screen time," "social comparison," and "cyberbullying." By the end of Week 4, a formal research question and a theoretical framework will be established.

**Phase II: Methodology and Ethics (Weeks 5–8)**
The researcher will design the data collection instruments, including a standardized survey and a set of semi-structured interview questions. During this phase, the researcher will submit the proposal to the Institutional Review Board (IRB) to ensure ethical compliance regarding minor participants. The researcher will also select a target demographic of teenagers aged 13–18.

**Phase III: Data Collection (Weeks 9–13)**
The researcher will distribute the surveys via school partnerships and social media platforms. Simultaneously, the researcher will conduct one-on-one interviews with a selected subset of participants. Data will be logged systematically, and the researcher will monitor for any signs of distress in participants, ensuring a safe environment for disclosure.

**Phase IV: Analysis and Synthesis (Weeks 14–15)**
The researcher will utilize statistical software to analyze quantitative survey data and thematic coding to interpret qualitative interview transcripts. The final weeks will be dedicated to synthesizing these findings into a comprehensive research paper, culminating in a final presentation of the correlation between specific social media behaviors and mental health outcomes.

### Option 2: First-Person Perspective
**Project Title:** *My Study on the Impact of Social Media on Teen Mental Health.*

**Phase I: Foundation and Literature Review (Weeks 1–4)**
I will start my semester by diving into the current research on how social media affects the teenage brain. I plan to read as many peer-reviewed journals as possible to understand the link between "likes" and self-esteem. By the end of the first month, I will have my research question finalized and a clear roadmap for how I want to approach my study.

**Phase II: Methodology and Ethics (Weeks 5–8)**
Next, I will focus on building my tools. I need to create a survey that is easy for my peers to understand but detailed enough to give me good data. I will also spend time drafting my ethics application; since I am studying minors, I have to be extremely careful about privacy and consent. I want to make sure my participants feel safe sharing their honest experiences with me.

**Phase III: Data Collection (Weeks 9–13)**
This is the most active part of my semester. I will be reaching out to local schools and using my own networks to get the word out about my survey. I also plan to sit down with a few students for one-on-one interviews. I want to hear their personal stories—not just numbers on a page. I will keep a close eye on the data as it comes in to ensure I’m capturing the nuances of their daily lives.

**Phase IV: Analysis and Synthesis (Weeks 14–15)**
In the final stretch, I will sit down with all the data I’ve gathered. I’ll use software to crunch the numbers from the surveys and look for recurring themes in my interviews. My goal is to connect the dots between what the data says and what the teenagers told me. I will spend the last two weeks polishing my final paper and preparing to present my findings to the class.
evaluation_result:
**Analysis**: The Model Response provides two versions of the semester-long research plan: one in the third-person perspective and another in the first-person perspective. However, the Instruction contains conflicting constraints, as it simultaneously requests the plan to be written entirely in the third-person perspective and entirely in the first-person perspective. The Model Response does not acknowledge this conflict and instead directly provides both versions without addressing or resolving the contradiction.

**Answer**: 1
